data.title: ETB: Hard To Recommend This Fund In A Bull Market
data.description:
The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund (NYSE:ETB) is an interesting closed-end fund that provides investors with a high level of current income and exposure to equities, while employing a fairly original strategy that provides a certain amount of protection against a market crash. I explained the advantages of investing in equities over fixed-income securities in a recent article. In short, fixed-income securities have failed to provide investors with protection against inflation, and both investment-grade bonds and even preferred stocks have delivered negative total real returns over the past ten years. Equities, on the other hand, have done much better and have managed to preserve investors’ purchasing power over the ten-year period. Unfortunately, though, equities have very low yields right now. We can see this in the following chart, which shows the yields of the major broad-market domestic equity indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

S&P 500 Index (SPY)

1.22%

NASDAQ-100 Index (QQQ)

0.58%

Russell 2000 Index (IWM)

1.20%

Dow Jones Industrial Average (DIA)

1.52%

As we can see, all of the major domestic broad-market indices have current yields of less than 2%. This is a disappointing and discouraging situation for anyone who is seeking to generate the income that they need to cover their expenses from their portfolios. After all, it is necessary to invest in equities in order to preserve the purchasing power of the investment principal, but the yields are so low that one cannot live comfortably off of the income that equities produce. A closed-end fund such as the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund can provide a possible solution to this problem.

As of the time of writing, the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund boasts a 9.22% current yield. Obviously, this is substantially higher than any of the broad-market domestic equity indices shown above. It also compares fairly well with other closed-end funds that employ similar strategies. We can see that in this chart:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund

Equity-Covered-Call Funds

9.22%

Virtus Dividend, Interest & Premium Strategy Fund (NFJ)

Equity-Covered-Call Funds

10.23%

BlackRock Enhanced Large Cap Core Fund (CII)

Equity-Covered-Call Funds

8.46%

Columbia Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund (STK)

Equity-Covered-Call Funds

6.45%

First Trust Enhanced Equity Income Fund (FFA)

Equity-Covered-Call Funds

7.14%

Voya Global Advantage and Premium Opportunity Fund (IGA)

Equity-Covered-Call Funds

10.18%

XAI Madison Equity Premium Income Fund (MCN)

Equity-Covered-Call Funds

12.06%

The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund is actually the median fund out of this grouping of seven peers, which is not a bad place to be. Naturally, investors could potentially obtain a higher level of income by purchasing shares of one of the higher-yielding peer funds. However, the fact that the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund does not have an outsized yield relative to its peers, and in fact is only the median out of its peer group, suggests that the market is not pricing in a distribution cut. We should still be sure to have a look at the fund’s financial statements and its distribution coverage, but the market does not seem to believe that a distribution cut is imminent, and this is a rather comforting place to begin.

With that said, the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund has failed to cover its distributions year-to-date. As we can see here, the fund’s net asset value has declined by 6.78% since the start of the year:

If the fund was fully covering its distribution, then its net asset value should be flat or positive. What we see here is different. Whenever the fund pays out a distribution, its net asset value declines because a distribution results in a capital outflow from the fund. If the fund’s portfolio were actually earning enough investment profits to cover these outflows, then we should see the fund’s net asset value increase in the days or weeks preceding the distribution, then decline whenever the distribution is paid. The fact that the fund’s net asset value has decreased year-to-date thus means that the fund is not generating sufficient investment profits to cover its distribution. This is certainly not a positive sign for anyone who is hoping to purchase the fund as a way of obtaining a safe and sustainable income to cover their lifestyle expenses.

One of the disadvantages of an option-writing strategy, such as the one that is employed by this fund, is that it reduces the potential gains from a common stock position. This could be problematic when it comes to preserving purchasing power. Inflation is, by definition, a decline in the value of the U.S. dollar (or another currency). Thus, any asset that aims to preserve purchasing power needs to increase in price when measured in U.S. dollar terms. The fact that the strategy of the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund puts an artificial cap on the capital gains that the portfolio can earn works against this requirement. Fortunately, the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund has still managed to maintain the purchasing power of an investor’s assets over time. This chart shows the total real returns of the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund assuming that all distributions paid were reinvested in shares of the fund after adjusting for inflation as measured by the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers over the past ten years:

As we can see, with these assumptions in place, the fund managed to deliver a total real return of 49.06% over the period in question, which works out to about 4.07% per year. Please note that this is the amount that investors received in excess of what was needed just to keep up with inflation.

Unfortunately, though, this could prove problematic for investors who need or want to use the fund entirely as a source of income. As we can see, the fund only delivered a total real return of 4.07% annually on average. The yield of this fund has historically been above 4%, so investors would need to reinvest some of the distributions that they received just to keep up with inflation. In short, only about half of the distributions can be taken as income if the maintenance of purchasing power is important. Roughly half of the distribution would need to be reinvested to keep up with inflation. This might reduce the appeal of the fund somewhat, but we should keep in mind that all of the fund’s peers would have this same problem. As regular readers are likely aware, I strongly expect inflation to be a bigger problem going forward than most of us are used to, so this could be important to keep in mind when constructing a portfolio.

Recent Performance Update

We previously discussed the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund on August 5, 2024. That was almost ten months ago, and as we all know, a lot can happen in ten months. The most recent ten-month period was certainly no exception to this as there were a number of things that occurred that had an impact on the equity markets, and by extension, the performance of this fund.

Perhaps surprisingly, one of the most important events to have impacted the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund was the January announcement from Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co. (DEEPSEEK) that it has developed a large language model for generative artificial intelligence that requires substantially less processing power than American generative artificial intelligence. This announcement prompted doubts among investors that the development and deployment of generative artificial intelligence would result in enormous demand for new hardware. As you may recall, the excitement around generative artificial intelligence was one of the driving factors in the equity markets up until this announcement. We can see this in the fact that the so-called “Magnificent 7” (MAGS), which is a group consisting of the largest technology companies in the United States, was up 66.52% in 2024 alone:

As clearly shown, the seven technology companies that comprise this grouping – Tesla (TSLA), Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), NVIDIA (NVDA), Amazon.com (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG), and Meta Platforms (META) – substantially outperformed the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the course of 2024. This shows just how excited the market was about generative artificial intelligence. As these seven companies are also among the largest in the United States in terms of market capitalization, this outperformance had a major impact on the S&P 500 Index over that year, to the point where these seven companies were almost entirely responsible for the strong performance that the entire index delivered in that year.

The release of Deepseek’s resource-light chatbot changed all that because it showed that generative artificial intelligence does not necessarily need expensive hardware infrastructure to function. Thus, there may not be as much demand for the specialized semiconductors that are being sold by NVIDIA as was previously priced into the company’s stock price. The market started to sell off the “Magnificent 7” once it realized this, and these companies as a group have underperformed the S&P 500 Index by a fairly significant amount year-to-date:

As we can see, the “Magnificent 7” are down 1.51% year-to-date compared to the S&P 500 Index, which is almost flat.

This is important for the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund because this fund has historically had very high exposure to the American technology sector. In the previous article, we saw that Microsoft, NVIDIA, Apple, Amazon.com, Alphabet, Meta Platforms, and Broadcom (AVGO) accounted for 35.94% of the fund’s assets as of June 30, 2024. These companies also accounted for eight (including both classes of Alphabet’s publicly-traded stock) of the fund’s ten largest positions. This is actually higher than what the S&P 500 Index had to these stocks on that date. As such, we can expect the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund to have been adversely affected by the weakness in the technology sector year-to-date. However, the fund likewise would have benefited from the strength in the technology sector during the latter months of 2024. However, its strategy prevents it from fully taking advantage of any strength in the market, so it would generally lag during a bull run such as the one in 2024. In theory, though, this same strategy will also result in the fund receiving option premiums even when the market is declining, which will partially offset the adverse consequences of market weakness. Its performance should, therefore, resemble that of the broader market, but with lower highs and shallower lows.

Thus, we can probably expect the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund to have delivered some gains to its investors over the August 2024 to December 2024 period, offset with some capital losses in 2025. This is, in fact, exactly what we do see:

This chart shows the price performance of the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund from August 5, 2024, through today. As we can see, the shares of the fund are up 2.68%, which is worse than the performance of the S&P 500 Index over the same period. In fact, the fund trailed the S&P 500 Index over the entire period. That is what we would expect given that the fund’s options strategy reduces its upside potential compared to the index, but it is still quite disappointing. Indeed, this might be discouraging to some investors.

The fund did, fortunately, manage to do a bit better on a total return basis:

As we can see, the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund managed to deliver a 10.59% total return over the approximately ten-month period. This was still worse than the total return of the S&P 500 Index (SP500TR) over the period, but we expect that given the fund’s options strategy. Overall, though, a 10.59% total return over a ten-month period is not bad at all, and it is in line with the historical long-term return of common stocks. Thus, investors probably should not be too disappointed here despite the fund underperforming the S&P 500 Index.

About The Fund

The fund’s website states that the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of current income and current gains. This is an unusual objective for an equity fund, given that equities do not provide much in the way of income to investors. As we saw in the introduction, the current yield of the S&P 500 Index is only 1.22%, which means that a $1 million investment would only generate $12,200 per year in annual income. It is highly unlikely that anyone who is able to invest a million dollars in any asset would be satisfied with such a minuscule level of income.

The low yield of common equities has been a problem for quite some time. For example, take a look at the S&P 500 Index compared to the S&P 500 Total Return Index over the past thirty years:

As we can see, the index increased in price by 1,009.05% over the thirty-year period. However, if an investor would have bought every component of the index thirty years ago and reinvested all of the dividends that were paid out by the constituent companies into the index, it would only increase the total return by 823.51% over a thirty-year period. In other words, the dividends plus all of the compounding that the dividends delivered over that period did not even double an investor’s total return over a full thirty years. Clearly, common equities are not a particularly good income vehicle.

We can further see that the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund is not generating much income from its portfolio by looking at the yields of the largest positions in the fund. As of April 30, 2025, these are the largest positions in the fund’s portfolio:

Here are the yields of the common stocks of these companies:

Company Name

Current Yield

Apple

0.52%

Microsoft

0.73%

NVIDIA

0.03%

Amazon.com

n/a

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B)

n/a

Meta Platforms

0.33%

Broadcom

0.99%

Alphabet Inc. – Class A (GOOGL)

0.49%

JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM)

2.13%

Alphabet Inc. – Class C

0.46%

JPMorgan Chase is the only company shown that has a yield in excess of 2%. In fact, nearly all of these companies have yields of less than 1%. When we consider that the fund’s portfolio is very top-heavy (the above ten stocks account for 37.63% of the overall portfolio), we can immediately see that the fund is not making very much money off of dividends. This makes it very difficult for it to achieve a current income objective from this portfolio alone.

Rather, what this fund is doing to generate income is selling call options against the S&P 500 Index. This provides the fund with an upfront source of income because whoever buys the written option has to pay a premium to the fund. The risk, though, is that the S&P 500 Index will rise and result in losses for the fund. The purpose of the stock portfolio is to offset these losses, and the strategy works pretty well as long as the stocks in the fund’s portfolio manage to match or beat the S&P 500 Index. However, this fund is only holding 154 common equities (far less than the index), so there is no guarantee that the portfolio will deliver comparable performance to the S&P 500 Index. This is the big risk inherent in the fund’s strategy. As this fund is writing index options as opposed to single-stock options like most other option-income funds do, it technically has greater risks because it does not own the underlying asset.

The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund is not the only one of Eaton Vance’s option-income funds that writes index options without owning the indices. Indeed, there are a few funds run by this manager that use such a strategy. One thing that sets this fund apart from some of the others, though, is that nearly all of its portfolio is overlaid with options. The fund’s most recent fact sheet states that it has index call options written against 95% of the value of its portfolio. Thus, the fund has essentially capped the capital gains that it can earn on nearly all of its portfolio. This is, it is worth noting, something that works against this fund during bull market periods. For example, over the past ten years, the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund has actually suffered from a price decline of 11.90% compared to a 181.37% gain in the S&P 500 Index:

The fund’s attractive yield cannot make up for this, as the inclusion of the distributions only brings the fund’s total return up to 103.44% over the period, less than the index.

The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund (ETY) employs a very similar strategy to this one. It assembles a very similar portfolio that consists of domestic common stocks and then writes index call options against the value of its portfolio. The key difference is that the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund relies much less heavily on the options for income. The fact sheet for that fund states that it only has options written against 48% of its portfolio. This gives the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund a substantial advantage over the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund in bull markets because it does not sacrifice as much upside potential. We can see the effects of this over the course of the past ten years, which has been a very strong bull market with only a few exceptions:

As we can see, the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund substantially underperformed its sister fund on a total return basis. The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund did have a slight advantage during market pullbacks, but such pullbacks tended to be very short-lived over this period. Over the long term, the fact that the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund was able to earn a higher proportion of the capital gains of the common stocks gave it a major advantage. We can expect this to be the case going forward, and as such, the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund is not as good of a holding as its sister fund for long-term investors.

The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund is the better of the two funds during market panics or bear markets, but it is difficult to see any risk of such an event in the near future. At this point, the market has already priced in any potential negative news that could arise from the ongoing efforts of the current U.S. leadership to reduce the country’s trade deficits, and most countries around the world seem more inclined to cooperate on renegotiating trade deals rather than start a trade war. Additionally, a weakening in the U.S. dollar should reduce the relative attractiveness of holding cash relative to other assets and potentially prompt investors to increase their common stock allocations at the expense of cash. Thus, it is hard to make a case that a bear market is on the horizon, and thus the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund is likely to be a suboptimal choice. Investors who like the basic strategy of this fund would probably be better off with the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund due to its greater potential for capital gains.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund is to provide its investors with a high level of current income. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a regular monthly distribution of $0.1058 per share ($1.2696 per share annually). This gives the fund a 9.22% yield at the current share price.

The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund has been pretty reliable with its distribution for a while. It cut back in October of 2022, but it has been increasing it ever since:

The fund’s current distribution has remained the same since April of 2024, when it was increased from the previous level. However, this does not necessarily mean that the fund can actually afford the distribution. As we saw in the introduction, its net asset value is down year-to-date, which strongly suggests that it has been failing to maintain it so far in 2025. Let us have a look at the annual report for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, to see how well it did in covering the distribution last year.

For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund reported a net investment income of $1,646,141, which was not nearly enough to cover the $36,183,774 that the fund paid out in distributions. Thus, the fund failed to fully cover the payout with its net investment income, but that is normal for equity funds, so it is probably not a big deal.

Unfortunately, the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund also failed to cover the distribution through realized gains. For the twelve-month period, it reported net realized gains of $24,521,032, which, even when added to the fund’s net investment income, was not sufficient to cover the distribution.

Overall, the fund’s net asset value did increase over the full-year period due to unrealized gains. However, since unrealized gains can be erased by market weakness, we may not want to rely on them. The fact that the fund’s net asset value is down year-to-date shows the problem with relying on unrealized gains to cover a distribution. However, it is worth noting that the fund’s net asset value today is 3.59% higher than it was at the start of 2024:

Thus, for now it appears that the fund managed to fully cover all of the distributions that it has paid out since the start of 2024. It has only been able to do so because its unrealized gains have been sufficient to offset the distributions that it paid out in excess of its net realized gains and net investment income. There is no guarantee that this will always be the case, but the fund does seem to be okay for now.

Valuation

Shares of the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund are currently trading at a 5.64% discount on net asset value. This is a less attractive price than the 7.15% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

Over the past three years, the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund has traded at prices ranging from an 11.91% discount to a 21.42% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 2.74% discount, so today’s price is more attractive than the three-year average.

Here is how the current price of this fund compares to its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund

(5.64%)

Virtus Dividend, Interest & Premium Strategy Fund

(11.13%)

BlackRock Enhanced Large Cap Core Fund

(6.07%)

Columbia Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund

4.65%

First Trust Enhanced Equity Income Fund

(4.32%)

Voya Global Advantage and Premium Opportunity Fund

(1.47%)

XAI Madison Equity Premium Income Fund

(2.92%)

The median price of this peer group is a 4.32% discount on net asset value, so we can see that the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund is currently priced at a better-than-average price when compared to its peer group. It is not the cheapest fund shown, though, as there are a few funds trading with somewhat larger discounts to their intrinsic values.

The current price of the fund does not appear to be too bad, considering that it is currently at a better price than its long-term average and is at a better price than its peers. It has had a more attractive price quite regularly over the past month, though.

Expenses

The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund has an expense ratio of 1.11%. This fund does not use leverage, so it does not have to pay any carrying costs or interest due to that.

Here is how this fund’s expense ratio compares to its peers:

Fund Name

Expense Ratio

Expense Ratio ex-Leverage Carrying Costs

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund

1.11%

1.11%

Virtus Dividend, Interest & Premium Strategy Fund

0.96%

0.96%

BlackRock Enhanced Large Cap Core Fund

0.93%

0.93%

Columbia Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund

1.13%

1.13%

First Trust Enhanced Equity Income Fund

1.12%

1.12%

Voya Global Advantage and Premium Opportunity Fund

1.00%

1.00%

XAI Madison Equity Premium Income Fund

1.09%

1.09%

(all figures from CEF Data)

The median expense ratio here is 1.09%, so the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund is unfortunately a bit more expensive than its peers. Investors who dislike high fees might not like this, but it is worth noting that this fund is not dramatically more expensive than the others in the group.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the strategy of the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund makes it a somewhat poor choice for long-term investors in a bull market. The fund underperforms the similar Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Diversified Equity Fund whenever equity prices are rising, and it is likely that equities will continue to go up going forward. This fund appears to be a very good choice during a flat or mild bear market, though, but given current conditions, it is difficult to recommend it.

data.title: Caleres: Higher Debt And Compressing Margins Don't Help In The Tariff Storm
data.description:
Caleres, Inc.'s (NYSE:CAL) Q1 '25 results continue to show deterioration.

Sales were down across both Brand Portfolio and Famous Footwear, and inventories rose significantly, particularly within the brand segment. At the same time, bad debt expenses and customer cancellations suggest a worsening outlook for wholesale.

Since my previous article in March, the stock is down 20%. However, guidance has now been withdrawn, margins have continued to deteriorate, and the company plans to increase debt to acquire Stuart Weitzman. I continue to believe Caleres is a Hold despite the optically low earnings ratio.

Q1 '25 results were bad and FY25 seems worse

Sales were down 6.9% for the company's Brand Portfolio and 6.3% in Famous Footwear (its retail operation). Within Brand, the decline was broad-based, across all names. Famous comps fell 4.6%, worsening from Q4 '24 comps of -2.9%.

Margins, however, were the bigger issue. Gross margin in Brand fell 280bps YoY, driven by reserves taken against excess fall and spring inventory (up 20% YoY for the segment). The company stated these inventories are current, and the reserves could reverse in future quarters. However, even if inventory values recover, the gross margin dilution could remain, as clearance prices will still impact profitability (the reserve is taken when the company believes it will sell the inventories below their purchase price, so that even if they are sold later, the gross margin is 0%). On top of that, Caleres recorded $3.1 million in bad debt expense related to deteriorating credit profiles in its wholesale customer base.

Further, the company said that its wholesale order book was “fluid” and that some customers canceled orders in Q1. This adds to the already challenging picture for Brand, which continues to shrink in both sales and margin contribution. In this context, Caleres is still preparing to acquire a new brand—Stuart Weitzman, even as all of its current brands but one are shrinking. The acquisition will add $105 million to net debt, despite rising risk across all parts of the business.

Retail was also negative, but slightly more resilient. Famous Footwear benefited from the initial rollout of Jordan product, and probably benefited from Nike's reorganization of its wholesale strategy. Still, Famous gross margins fell 80bps due to promotions and freight, and segment operating margins came in at only 1.5%.

It’s also worth noting that competition in the U.S. family footwear market could increase. Dick’s Sporting Goods (DKS) recently acquired Foot Locker, potentially intensifying competitive pressure. Q2-to-date sales for Famous remain negative at -4.5% (according to the earnings call).

Valuation is still unattractive

Because of tariff volatility and despite recent signals of a potential U.S.–China reset, Caleres suspended its guidance for FY25. This is likely the right move given how unstable the situation remains.

If we assume a similar trajectory to 1Q25 for the remainder of the year, i.e., revenues down 5% and further compression in margins, then full-year operating income could fall to ~$110 million (4% operating margin, in line with long-term averages for the company). Although this represents a significant 30% decline in operating income from FY24, readers should notice that 1Q24 operating profits fell by 60% YoY.

Interest expense remains contained at ~$16 million annually, but this is before the $105 million in new debt needed to finance the Stuart Weitzman acquisition. At a 7–8% interest rate, this could add ~$8 million annually in interest costs.

That implies FY25 net income could fall to the $50–60 million range, assuming sales stabilize. If not, the situation could worsen.

At a $440 million market cap (versus ~$550 million in March when I last wrote about the company), Caleres trades at 7–9x forward earnings. This may appear low, but this is a shrinking business, with collapsing margins, rising debt, and high exposure to the U.S. consumer, a sector likely to come under stress later in 2025.

While the optical multiple is low, the fundamental Caleres, Inc. picture remains unattractive. I maintain my Hold.

data.title: QQQ Could Move Much Lower
data.description:
The NASDAQ 100 (NASDAQ:QQQ) has rallied sharply after taking a big hit in April following Liberation Day, and it is up 1.9% now year-to-date. Those that own it should exit in my view.

A Look at QQQ

The QQQ ETF, managed by Invesco, launched in 1999, just ahead of the top of the NASDAQ 100 and in a big bull market. It tracks the NASDAQ-100 Index, which was created in early 1985.

The NASDAQ-100, hosted by NASDAQ and 40 years old, has been an incredibly strong performer since inception. The goal of the index is to capture the 100 largest non-financial stocks on the NASDAQ.

Today's index is extremely large, with a current market cap of over $25 trillion. QQQ represents only $324 billion, a little over 1%. QQQ is also highly concentrated, with the top 10 names accounting for 50% of the index:

Looking at it by sector allocation, there are two very large components, Technology and Consumer Discretionary.

The QQQ Chart Suggests It Has Topped

Since the pandemic lows, QQQ has soared, rising in price by 173.6% since the end of March in 2020:

Since that same date, the S&P 500 has increased in price by almost 125%, so QQQ has outpaced the overall market. Going back to the 2016 elections, QQQ has soared 334.8%, almost double the price-return of the S&P 500.

Looking at it from the bottom of the bear market in 2009, it has increased more than 1900%, more than double the return of the S&P 500:

QQQ has rallied immensely over the past 16 years, but it hasn't always been straight up. The recent pullback was more than more than 25%, and the pandemic pullback was 24%. Of course, there was a sharper pullback from the all-time high set after the pandemic, with QQQ falling over 35% into early 2023.

Shorter-term, reasonable observers can argue about whether the plunge in April was just a correction, but I think that the pullback from the all-time high set after the election is the start of a bear market. QQQ posted an all-time high of 540.81 during trading on 2/19, when it closed at 539.52. At 521.22 currently, it is down 3.6% from that level.

To me, the chart looks very toppy:

Three open gaps capture my attention, including the one set on 5/27 from 511.84, the one set 5/12 from 491.54 and the one set on 4/23 from 447.58 QQQ has recovered its 150-day moving average as well.

From my perspective, bulls were quite optimistic after the elections in November. QQQ closed at 492.21 on 11/5, and it is currently up 5.9% since then. Of course, it is down from the subsequent all-time high set in February by 3.6%. My expectation technically is that QQQ could test the lows of late 2022 (260 area), and this would represent a decline of 50%. It would still be well above the 2020 low of 165. For those who aren't aware that QQQ can fall 50% or more, the decline in the early 2000s was greater.

Looking at other parts of the market since the elections, U.S. Treasuries are sharply higher now despite further cuts to Fed Funds by the FOMC. The 2-year, currently at 3.97%, has dropped from 4.19%, but the 30-year, currently at 4.95%, has increased from 4.44%. The 10-year has increased from 4.28% to 4.45%.

Stocks have been mixed. While QQQ has rallied almost 6%, the S&P 500 has gained only 2.5%. Small-caps, as measured by the Russell 2000 Index, have declined by 7.6%. I was very bearish in early April, but I initiated coverage on iShares Russell 2000 (IWM) as a buy on 4/6. I was premature in downgrading it to Hold later than month.

By the way, the top 10 stocks in QQQ are mixed year-to-date, with two double-digit gainers and two double-digit decliners:

The QQQ Valuation Is Quite High

Figuring the valuation of QQQ is not that simple. The right way, in my view, is to take each of the 100 members and weight their valuations. This can be challenging if some are losing money.

Invesco's QQQ website does not cover valuation much. It mentions on the quarterly fact sheet the PE as being 34.1X. It doesn't cover enterprise value to adjusted EBITDA.

The top 10 names that account for about half of QQQ, and they seem quite expensive to me. I am short through ETFs Nvidia (NVDA) and Tesla (TSLA) and don't care for them at all. Here is the entire group with enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA levels (per Koyfin):

  • Tesla: 67X
  • Netflix: 35X
  • Costco: 33X
  • Broadcom: 26X
  • Nvidia: 26X
  • Apple: 21X
  • Microsoft: 20X
  • Meta Platforms: 13X
  • Amazon: 13X
  • Google: 11X

The average stock by this metric is 26.5X, and the weighted average works out to be 27.9X. No thanks! The growth rates in adjusted EBITDA for these companies are much lower, and these valuations are much higher than the typical stock. Some have free cash flow generation, but the stocks trade at a very high multiple to their free cash flow.

In my view, big stocks in QQQ are likely to trade at an enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA of 20X or less. The estimates could come down too. The big problem is that the U.S. debt has exploded higher and is a threat to our government and, ultimately, to our businesses. The customers of the companies in QQQ are struggling and will continue to struggle.

Conclusion

QQQ has rallied sharply since plunging in early April and is up now slightly year-to-date. The ETF has 50% exposure to just 10 names, which look expensive. I am short QQQ through a position in ProShares UltraPro Short QQQ ETF (SQQQ), and I expect QQQ to fall a lot over the balance of the year.

data.title: Caleres: Looks Cheap, But Losing Its Solid Footing
data.description:
When Caleres, Inc. (NYSE:CAL) announced its intention to acquire Stuart Weitzman in February, I was both surprised and interested to learn more about this deal.

Shares rose on the back of the news that the company acquired the troubling brand in a $105 million deal from Tapestry (TAP), yet with the deal on a net basis increasing leverage and adding unprofitable operations to Caleres, it created some risks as well, even as the valuation looked dirt cheap.

I feared the current situation in February, as the business is hit hard by the tariff discussions and anticipated leverage overhang from the Stuart Weitzman deal, making me cautious to go bottom fishing yet.

Shoes

Caleres was founded in 1878 and is a merchant-driven shoe business which includes a line-up of powerful brand assets, with strength in sourcing, logistics, marketing and technology. Some of these brands include Vionic, Vince, Franco Sarto, LifeStride, among many others.

The business has been posting stable sales over the past decade, but amidst a growing economy and impact of inflation, that suggests that the business has been losing relative relevance. A $2.6 billion business a decade ago saw sales peak in 2023 at levels near $3.0 billion, after which revenues have fallen to about $2.7 billion here. Promising is that despite sluggish sales trends is that operating margins typically have come in a mid-single, to high-single digits.

In the meantime, Caleres has been able to buy back about a fifth of its shares, which meant that some earnings per share growth has been seen. I picked up the valuation with the release of the 2023 results (as released early in 2024) with sales reported down 5% to $2.8 billion, as operating profits fell by twice that percentage to $194 million.

GAAP earnings of $170 million came in at $4.80 per share, although that adjusted earnings were reported at $4.18 per share. Important to understand is that the company has a $1.6 billion mainstream Famous Footwear segment, which comprises over 800 stores, while the Brand Portfolio generates nearly $1.3 billion store, but it does so with about 100 stores.

With earnings power trending at $4 per share, a $40 stock early in 2024 looked to be fairly valued, amidst a modest net debt load. Shares, however, fell as the guidance for stable performance in 2024, had to be re-called during the year. After no less than 3 reductions in the full-year guidance, the company guided for 2024 sales down 3.0-3.5%, with adjusted earnings seen around $3.25 per share.

Nonetheless, a $17 stock in February traded at a very compelling 5 times earnings multiple, even as these earnings were set to come in a dollar short of original 2024 expectations.

Valuation & Dealmaking

With 34 million shares trading at $17 at the start of the year, the equity value of Caleres had fallen to $578 million, for a $780 million enterprise valuation if we factor in $204 million in net debt. This net debt load was perfectly manageable amidst EBITDA reported at $237 million.

Given this backdrop, a $105 million deal to acquire Stuart Weitzman was a bit surprising, even after seller Tapestry bought the business a decade ago for roughly 5 times that amount. With a $241 million revenue contribution, the deal would contribute nearly 10% to sales, yet Tapestry reported a $21 million segment loss for the business.

Shares of Caleres moved up 5%, yet pro forma net debt would jump to $310 million, all while EBITDA would come down amidst the losses taken on. It was quite clear that this is a promising brand to added to the line-up, yet real execution is required. All this made that I liked the dirt cheap valuation, but was not willing to commit to the shares yet, looking for more clues about the deal.

Coming Down

Since February, shares of Caleres have gradually come down to $13 and change at this point in time, as the company has seen tough operating conditions driven by the tariffs fears.

In March, Caleres reported its 2024 results. Fourth quarter sales were down over 8%, making that full-year sales were down 3.4% to $2.73 billion, hardly inspiring numbers. Adjusted earnings fell from $4.18 to $3.30 per share, as net debt ticked down below $200 million ahead of the Stuart Weitzman deal.

Full-year sales were seen flattish for 2025 with adjusted earnings seen at a midpoint $3.00 per share. While this is not too inspiring after a tough 2024, it looked comforting given the tariffs discussions. However, the company guided for a soft start to the year, with first quarter sales seen down 5-6%. Both guidances were excluding the Stuart deal, seen closing in the summer

Soon thereafter, the company paid out a $0.07 per share quarterly dividend now yielding about 2%. While that is not too inspiring, it marks consecutive dividends having been paid out for more than 100 years in a row!

By the end of May, Caleres announced a 6.8% fall in first quarter sales to $614 million and change, with sales coming in softer than anticipated, as uncertainty triggered the company in withdraw the full-year guidance. The decline in sales weighted heavily on margins with operating profits falling from $42 million and change to $11 million and change, as GAAP earnings are down three-quarters to $0.21 per share.

Part of the severe margin pressure for the first quarter, which ended early in May, was due to the decision of the company to halt production during the quarter in China. This decision was made in April, resulting in some unidentified costs incurred during the quarter. Net debt ticked up to $225 million, in part because of built-up in inventories, resulting in very poor cash flow generation.

Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was cut in half to $28 million and change, important given the pending deal for Stuart. If annualized, this results in EBITDA just over $100 million, while pro forma net debt will increase to more than $300 million here, and that is while Stuart Weitzman initially will add only losses to the firm. Trying to offset some of these headwinds, the company announced cost-cutting plans, designed to reduce selling, general & administrative expenses by around $15 million per annum.

Not Touching

Frankly, I have been surprised how well shares of Caleres held up during the tariff discussions, as quite some bad news was priced into the stock, of course. It is clear that the business is suffering, and that debt related concerns are rising rapidly amidst increased debt, but moreover poor earnings, and the to-be-added losses from Stuart Weitzman.

All this makes me very cautious and still in no rush to get involved with the shares here, making me patiently await more clarity before reconsidering this stance.

data.title: Nvidia: Time To Get Greedy
data.description:
Thursday’s Q1 2026 earnings presentation of Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) highlighted that the chip company is able to strive even in a challenging market.

In the last couple of months, Nvidia and other chip companies, like Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), have suffered from compressing valuation multiples as the U.S. made it harder to export chips to China.

Nvidia’s earnings presentation, however, proved that the bullish investment case for the chip company is fully intact, despite Nvidia taking a margin hit related to new export licensing requirements.

In my last piece on Nvidia, I pointed to the chip company’s aggressive profit up-scaling, catalyzed by the data center segment, as the main reason why investors should load up on Nvidia.

Moreover, Nvidia achieved record results in Gaming and AI PC due to robust demand for the chip company’s GeForce graphics cards.

In my view, Nvidia continues to deserve a stock classification of "Strong Buy."

Nvidia’s Business Remains In Excellent Shape

Nvidia’s Q1 2026 sales rose 69% YoY to $44.1 billion with data center sales once again catalyzing this growth: The data center segment produced a total of $39.1 billion in sales, up 73% YoY, primarily as artificial intelligence demands require companies to upgrade their chips.

While the data center business performed exceptionally well, as anticipated, the Gaming and AI PC segment, which has kind of taken a back seat to Nvidia’s data center in the last couple of quarters, reported record sales ($3.8 billion) as well.

Due to strong demand for GeForce graphics cards, Gaming and AI PC produced $3.8 billion in Q1 2026 sales, up 42 YoY. Though the segment is only producing sales one-tenth the size of Nvidia’s data center segment, it is good to see that Nvidia has momentum in more than just one part of its business.

Moreover, with Nvidia announcing a new slate of GeForce products in April, such as the GeForce RTX 5060, the chip company is possibly set to maintain its sales momentum moving forward as well.

Due to changes in U.S. trade policy that seeks to limit China’s access to high-performance graphics processing units, which can be used to train large-language models, the U.S. government introduced new export licensing requirements in Q2, dealing a serious blow to chip companies including Nvidia.

As a consequence, Nvidia was forced to write down the value of its excess chip inventory, which predominantly consists of H20 chips. Thus, Nvidia recognized an impairment of $4.5 billion in Q1 2026. This in turn caused a substantial QoQ margin drop, with operating profit margins falling for the first time since Q1 2024 below 50%: On a QoQ basis, Nvidia’s operating profit margin decreased 12.0 percentage points whereas its gross margins slumped 12.5 percentage points.

Though Nvidia has taken a considerable margin hit in Q1 2026 due to the U.S. changing its trade approach to China, the company is still growing its operating profits: In Q1 2026 Nvidia produced $21.6 billion in operating profits, up 28% YoY.

While changing U.S. trade policy and this administration's no-holds-barred approach to negotiation represents a headwind for the chip industry at large, Nvidia has proven that it can weather those storms while maintaining high operating profitability.

Moreover, companies are still very much invested in preparing for the AI age and are anticipated to scale up their spending moving forward.

Data Spending Runway Will Last Years

There have been concerns in the market earlier this year that data center-related spending patterns may not be as strong as originally estimated. However, new data suggests that companies are not backing away from spending heavily on AI infrastructure, particularly servers, which is Nvidia’s core business.

Based on data provided by the International Data Corporation, server spending is anticipated to skyrocket in the coming years, and expected to reach more than $200 billion by 2028. Goldman Sachs Research also supports the idea that the market is poised to see substantive growth in the years ahead.

In a February piece, analysts at Goldman Sachs Research estimated that AI will drive power demand up by 165% by the end of the decade. The reason: A steady new supply of data centers that need to be equipped with the most capable graphics processing units.

For companies like Nvidia, which provide the core product for data companies, GPUs, growing investments in AI/server infrastructure pose a favorable, long-term business tailwind that Nvidia is well-positioned to take advantage of.

Multiple Compression Highlights Buying Opportunity

Nvidia stock is quite affordable at 24x 2026 estimated profits taking into consideration that the chip company is anticipated to grow its earnings by 30% next year (profit estimates have also begun to rise after the chip company’s first quarter earnings presentation).

In my view, Nvidia can re-rate to a 30x 2026e profit multiple, which reflects back to us an implied intrinsic value of $171 per share. Since profit estimates are rising, there is upside in my stock price target as well.

Advanced Micro Devices, which is presently in the process of scaling up shipments of its new instinct AI accelerator series, is anticipated to grow its profits 45% next year and the stock of AMD is substantially cheaper than Nvidia as well as the semiconductor peer group average Price-To-Earnings multiple of 25.0x.

Intel Corp. (INTC) is anticipated to enjoy the fastest profit growth next year, which is primarily due to the chip company’s ongoing reorganization which is expected to yield structural cost savings.

Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), a fast-growing hardware/server company seeing enormous momentum in its business thanks to an unhinged AI spending spree, is actually the most expensive chip company in the peer group, selling for 30.6x 2026e profits.

In the case of Nvidia, I think the chip company made a very convincing investment case for itself with its Q1 2026 earnings presentation. Operating profits are still very much skyrocketing, and so are its sales, particularly in the data center segment. As far as I am concerned, the relatively low valuation multiple is a very good reason for investors to become more aggressive and gobble up the chip company’s stock.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Not Work For Investors

Nvidia has obviously seen considerable pressure on its operating margins, which I consider to be a negative, for obvious reasons. Despite the decrease in operating and gross margins, however, Nvidia remained very profitable and produced $21.6 billion in operating profits as well as $18.8 billion in net profits. With steady growth in data center supply anticipated until 2030, demand tailwinds should continue to be an uplifting factor for Nvidia.

My Takeaway

I often double down when a stock moves out of favor with investors (which I did when Nvidia’s stock sold for $107) primarily because I think in such a situation alpha return opportunities reveal themselves.

Nvidia’s operating margins took a substantial hit in Q1 2026 which is not due to softening demand or other structural issues in the chip company’s business. Rather, it is the U.S. government which required U.S. companies to seek an export license for high-performance chips with forced $4.5 billion in impairments on Nvidia in the last quarter.

The margin issue aside, Nvidia did very well in Q1 2026, particularly in data centers where the company is seeing no spending fatigue at all. Thus, Nvidia’s bullish investment case got a vital shot in the arm on Thursday, and I continue to see substantial upside potential for the chip company long term.

data.title: Taysha: TSHA-102 Presses On With IND Part B Protocol And SAP Amendments
data.description:
The last time I wrote an article on Taysha Gene Therapies, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSHA) it in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Taysha: Initial Rett Syndrome Data Leads To Other 2024 Catalysts." Since then, the company has been able to achieve two key positive developments relating to this program. The first of which is that it gained alignment with the FDA that a single-arm Part B study of TSHA-102 would be enough to file for regulatory approval of this gene therapy.

The second item being that it has also provided an update on data from Part A of its phase 1/2 REVEAL study, which was using this therapy to treat these Rett Syndrome patients. What remains now, which the company hopes to achieve in Q2 of 2025, is to submit the pivotal Part B protocol and SAP as an amendment to the original IND application. If the FDA agrees that these amendments are adequate, then the company believes it could initiate this pivotal trial using TSHA-102 to treat these Rett Syndrome patients in Q3 of 2025.

What I will be showing below is that the company was able to achieve an important efficacy endpoint in Part A of the phase 1/2 REVEAL study that will also be used in the soon-to-be expected pivotal trial initiation. With further positive results released from Part A of this study using TSHA-102 as a gene therapy to treat these Rett Syndrome patients, plus the catalyst of a trial initiation possibly happening in the latter part of this year, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

TSHA-102 Accomplishes Huge Feat Compared To Natural History Study Data

Taysha Gene Therapies is continuing to deliver with respect to the development of its main clinical program, which is the use of TSHA-102 as a gene therapy to treat patients with Rett Syndrome. As a matter of fact, it has been able to report additional positive Part A data from its ongoing phase 1/2 REVEAL study for this program and has even gone on to gain alignment with the FDA on a protocol plan for Part B to be a pivotal one.

Before going over either of these two new positive developments, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being. Rett Syndrome is characterized as a type of rare, neurodevelopmental disorder whereby girls primarily lose their previously learned skills and abilities. This is a highly devastating disorder because it is not just the ability to lose thinking capabilities. Such patients lose or don't develop the ability to speak, eat, breathe or perform other highly important tasks.

It is not as huge a market opportunity as many others, but it is a huge unmet medical need. The global market for Rett Syndrome in the 7 major markets [7MM] is expected to reach $441.5 million by 2035. Another important factor to mention is that this neurodevelopmental disorder primarily affects females. What's even more devastating about Rett Syndrome is that at some point in these patients' lives, they need caregiver support. What's impressive, which I will be going over into more detail below, is that the company was able to show that each patient was able to gain developmental milestones again.

In order to see whether or not this AAV9-directed gene therapy TSHA-102 is going to be able to be used effectively in helping to treat these patients with Rett Syndrome, the company ran an extensive phase 1/2 REVEAL program. In essence, it split up this program into two separate trials depending upon the age of the population. There was a phase 1/2 Pediatric study deployed to evaluate this gene therapy to treat these patients and see if this could actually be used as a one-time intrathecal administration therapy to help treat them. On top of a phase 1/2 Adult study that was deployed to recruit Adolescent and Adult patients.

As I indicated above, the patients recruited into the trial were all female. What was highly impressive is that the company was able to show that either two doses of intrathecal administration of this gene therapy was able to help these patients. In essence, the efficacy data established was based on a cutoff date of May 19th, 2025 whereby 10 females were given either a low-dose or high-dose of TSHA-102. The dosing separation that occurred was as follows:

  • 6 patients given low-dose 5.7x10^14 total vg TSHA-102
  • 4 patients given high-dose 1x1015 total vg TSHA-102.

Thus, 10 females with Rett Syndrome across both REVEAL phase 1/2 studies were evaluated. The findings achieved are nothing short of amazing because it was noted that all 100% of these patients had achieved one or more developmental milestones.

Why the heck is this an important finding? This goes back to what I noted directly above, in that these patients have to be provided with caregivers. Well, if they establish developmental milestones, then they can reduce burden on caregivers. Plus, they improve their very own quality-of-life [QoL] as well in the process.

The positive data update across both of these phase 1/2 Pediatric and Adult Rett Syndrome studies was one of the key developments I wanted to go over in terms of an update for continued advancement of TSHA-102. Another important development and why I believe it was important to go over this biotech again is because of what it was able to do with respect to its regulatory advancement. Following discussions with the FDA under Regenerative Medicines Advanced Therapy [RMAT] program consideration, it gained written alignment for making Part B of the phase 1/2 REVEAL program a pivotal study. A single-arm, open-label portion trial using TSHA-102 to treat a total of 15 females with Rett Syndrome will be enough for the company to file for U.S. marketing approval.

The biotech won't need to do much heavy lifting, but that doesn't mean that there isn't any risk in place. That's because the FDA noted that it should submit a pivotal Part B trial protocol along with a statistical analysis plan [SAP] to modify the IND application to begin the pivotal program. I want to state here that what is a positive about this is that it entirely avoided the need to have an end-of-phase 1/2 meeting. Instead, it just gets to submit both of these pieces for the IND. The written agreement it received pretty much lays out that the primary endpoint is that of obtaining one or more developmental milestone typically seen in a plateau patient population. Speaking of which, this was noted again in 100% of the patients who took AAV9 gene therapy TSHA-102. On the other hand, it was noted to be a 0% likelihood of being achieved in the natural history study. Each of the 15 patients to be recruited into this trial will serve as their own control.

If all goes well in terms of the protocol and SAP being submitted to the FDA, then the company should initiate the pivotal trial study for this program using TSHA-102 in Q3 of 2025. One item to mention is that the FDA also requested another item to be incorporated as part of the primary endpoint, which should lend credibility to the responder definition as part of such a pivotal trial. Along with the fact that there must be video proof of gain/regain of developmental milestone by independent, blinded central raters.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Taysha Gene Therapies had cash and cash equivalents of $116.6 million as of March 31st of 2025. It noted that it had enough cash on hand for a cash runway to fund its operations into Q4 of 2026. Despite this cash runway, it still decided to raise funds. With that being said, today, it announced the pricing of a public offering of common stock and warrants. The total gross proceeds to be obtained through this specific offering is expected to be about $200 million before deducting expenses. This offering is expected to close on or about May 30th of 2025.

The company burns approximately $23.8 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $15.6 million in R&D expenses and then $8.2 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Taysha Gene Therapies. The first risk to consider would be with respect to the expected Part B protocol and SAP to be submitted as part of amendments to the IND application to the FDA. The risk here is that even if the company is able to submit both of these to this U.S. agency, there is no assurance that the FDA will allow the company to begin the pivotal part B study of TSHA-102 for the treatment of patients with Rett Syndrome.

The second risk to then consider would be Part B of this pivotal trial. Even though this company was able to show that its AAV9-gene therapy allowed patients to achieve one or more gain/regain of developmental milestone, there is no assurance this is going to be reproduced again in this pivotal trial. In the event that this primary endpoint is achieved, along with another endpoint for responder definition, there is no assurance that TSHA-102 is going to be approved for U.S. marketing of this Rett Syndrome patient population.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that this company finds itself in. The reason why is because despite having an extended cash runway out to Q4 of 2026, it still chose to raise funds regardless. This is a huge risk because of the enormous amount of shares to be released, which should have a dilutive effect on the stock. As I noted directly above in the "Financials" section, it doesn't expect to complete this offering until about May 30th off 2025.

Conclusion

Taysha Gene Therapies has done well to advance its development of AAV9-directed TSHA-102 for the treatment of patients with Rett Syndrome. Especially, when you consider the fact that it was able to bypass an end-of-phase 2 [EOP2] meeting altogether. In essence, it just needs to complete the submission of its Part B protocol plan and SAP to the FDA so that it could get phase 1/2 Part B trial initiated. It expects to begin this pivotal study program in Q3 of 2025, and hopefully, it achieves the primary endpoint of gain/regain so that it could obtain approval. What's also highly ideal is that it intends to potentially release data at an earlier time point. This is going to be possible thanks to the plan to have a 6-month interim analysis performed for this pivotal Part B study along with a 12-month primary analysis as well.

data.title: Evolv Technologies: Could Expedite Lessen The Product Skepticism?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Evolv Technologies (NASDAQ:EVLV) has experienced a drastic increase in its stock price post the release of its earnings report on the 20th of May. The company has resolved its issues with the FTC, which allowed K-12 education customers to prematurely cancel their contracts with Evolv. Even though the SEC investigation involving the way it was recognizing its revenue is ongoing, the company is now in full compliance with the SEC and has filed all the delayed financial reports. With the release of the latest product, Evolv Expedite, the company might be starting off on the right track. However, the stock price seems to be overvalued relative to peers given the reduction in cash reserves and more and more contracts coming up for renewal; this doesn't present a good buying opportunity. I caution investors to wait for a price reversal and for the company to prove the effectiveness of its product offering.

Company Background

Evolv Technologies develops AI-powered weapon detectors that screen for dangerous weapons through the use of sensors and classification models in crowded environments like schools, hospitals, and arenas. The innovation behind Evolv's product is that, unlike traditional metal detectors where an inspector is required to look at the screen for an object that might look like a gun or knife, Evolv uses AI models for that.

Thanks to convolutional neural networks, most computers are now capable of image recognition. This innovation started by training machines to recognize dog and cat images, and now, with the assistance of sensors, they are capable of detecting concealed weapons and explosives. Evolv is one of the companies that is utilizing this breakthrough to bring safety to schools and hospitals. However, this technology is far from perfect; there are certain areas where false positives and false negatives occur. Classification models can be easily tricked by the shape of an object, which could result in false detection and sometimes failing to detect certain weapons.

Evolv Express is Evolv's flagship product that is AI-powered to detect concealed weapons without individuals having to form a queue and remove their bags. Because this technology solely relies on machines for detection, it can process a high volume of people quickly, reducing wait times and congestion at entry points.

The company monetizes this product through a subscription-based model that it brands as security-as-a-service (SAAS). The company leases its technology (hardware + software) to customers for a minimum period of 4 years. Prior to this model, Evolv would sell the product to customers and then offer maintenance & software updates as additional services. This limited its customer base since it required high upfront capital from customers and often resulted in lumpy revenues.

Evolv Express has faced major criticisms for failing to detect certain weapons like large knives and giving false alarms of umbrellas and Chromebooks, detecting them as guns. This is one major flaw with this technology, and even though this problem will get resolved as the training data improves, we are not sure when that will happen. Evolv announced to have resolved its issues with the FTC. The resolution didn't involve any monetary relief from the company but ordered it to allow an early contract cancellation from its K-12 education customers. The company was able to retain 92% of those customers and retained 90% of the deployed units. Also, some of those customers expanded their unit deployment, driving 92% in net revenue retention and 94% in net unit retention.

Evolv Expedite

Even though one of the advantages of Evolv Express is that individuals are not required to take off their bags for screening, the product often fails to detect certain weapons (mostly knives) concealed in bags. I get this criticism. If you are going to rely on a classification model to detect an object like a knife inside a backpack, there has to be the condition that there aren't many metal objects in that bag since that introduces noise in the data and could potentially lead to the model missing certain signals, like the shape of the knife.

To combat this problem, the company launched Evolv Expedite. A weapons detection system solely designed for screening bags, it works like a traditional X-ray scanner, but instead of needing a person to inspect the X-ray images for weapons, it uses AI for that. Unlike Evolv Express, which uses sensors to capture data and AI to interpret the image data, Expedite uses X-rays for data capture and an AI for interpretation.

I will admit that this recent product does compromise a little bit on the customer experience since it will result in lower throughput, but it will significantly increase the security and the safety of the venue. During the recent earnings call, Evolv reported having added a total of 12 customers that use Expedite. So this product is still in early stages, so we just have to watch and see the effectiveness of it as time goes on.

Earnings Results

Income Statement

For the first quarter of 2025, Evolv recorded $32 million in revenue, up by 44% relative to last year's first quarter and 10% sequentially. These revenue earnings beat estimates by $4 million, fueled by a 56% increase in product revenue, a 35% increase in subscription revenue, a 28% increase in services revenue, and a 205% increase in license fees YOY. Cost of revenue increased by 28%, resulting in a 58% increase in gross profits to a value of $19 million. G&A increased by 27%, sales and marketing decreased by 30%, R&D decreased by 24%, and the company incurred restructuring costs of $2.7 million. This resulted in a 2% decline in operational expenses and a 34% decrease in operating loss. Net loss was $1.7 million, beating GAAP EPS estimates by $0.08 and normalized EPS by $0.03.

Comparing this with the annual performance of 2024, we can see that the subscription revenue had a significant contribution to 2024 revenue results, up by 80% from 2023. Annual recurring revenue grew by 34% YoY to $106 million at the end of Q1 2025. The company further reported a positive adjusted EBITDA of $1.7 million in the first quarter and guided for revenue growth of 20%-25% in 2025.

Balance Sheet

Evolv continues to burn cash as it aggressively expands its product offering and spends more and more on sales and marketing. It's worth noting that in 2024, Evolv spent $117.9 million in G&A, sales, and marketing. That is $14 million higher than the revenue it earned. Cash & Equivalents decreased by 57%, and Accounts Receivable increased by 57%, bringing an overall decrease of 27% in current assets, while current liabilities increased by 18%. Which was significantly influenced by a 41% increase in accrued expenses and a 34% increase in the current portion of deferred revenue YoY. Overall, total assets declined by 9%, while total liabilities declined by 4%, resulting in a 15% decrease in shareholder's equity.

Even though the company doesn't have any outstanding debt, the state of this balance sheet does concern me a little bit. It looks like the company might find itself with negative working capital if it continues to deplete its cash reserves while accruing more expenses and deferred revenue. Since March 2021, Evolv's current portion of deferred revenue has increased from $3.1 million to $65.5 million to date. This shows that more lease contracts are nearing maturity and will need to be renewed. The company reported that 400 of the 6600 units are coming up for renewal this year.

Competitive Advantage & Valuation

The competitive advantage of this product lies within the AI model that powers the detection device. Companies with better training data will outperform those without. That proprietary data serves as a competitive advantage for Evolv, since Evolv is the leader in this space with more units deployed; this means it is outpacing its competition when it comes to data collection. Evolv's system screens more than 850,000 students and visitors on a daily basis. In Q1 nearly a dozen healthcare customers were added, totaling a 500 installation base in hospitals.

EVLV trades at a premium valuation relative to its peers on a TTM price-to-sales multiple. While SoundThinking (SSTI) and Xtract One Technologies (OTCQX:XTRAF) trade at 1.7 and 5.6, EVLV trades at 8.13.

This valuation comes after the company released impressive Q1 results, showing strong growth in subscription revenue and high retention from K-12 education customers. About 50% of the units & ARR booked came from existing customers; this indicates the sticky nature of this product, showing that if Evolv continues with aggressive deployment of its units, it will eventually corner this market. The Quant system rated EVLV a B for valuation at the beginning of this year, and after the earnings results, the system has given EVLV a D+ rating, signaling that the company might be slightly overvalued now.

Conclusion

Evolv is an interesting company with an innovative solution to the problem faced by most schools regarding violence and gun shootings. Evolv Express has shown its effectiveness in detecting firearms and other explosive objects, but the product has shown certain limitations in its capabilities, which then resulted in the company releasing another product, Evolv Expedite, to circumvent Express's shortcomings. Product-market fit is hard, and it's still early to tell if customers are willing to add expedite to their units for additional security. Meanwhile, cash reserves are depleting and contracts are nearing maturity. It might not be smart to hit the buy button for now; I advise investors to wait for a good valuation while tracking Evolv's contract renewals and product adoption rate. Hence, I rate this stock a hold.

data.title: Bitcoin's Just Another Asset
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Why Clem Chambers, CEO of Online Blockchain plc, is a huge optimist on blockchain, but not on the established players (0:20). Blockchain more interesting than Bitcoin (4:10). Not a fan of the ETFs (6:15). This is an excerpt from a recent episode.

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Clem Chambers, it's always great to talk to you on Seeking Alpha. Welcome back to Investing Experts. Great to have you.

Clem Chambers: Yeah, great to be on.

RS: So what would you say regarding the blockchain? What are some points of light for you? What are some companies or strategies that you feel like are doing well?

CC: I'm a huge optimist on blockchain, and I'm not a very big optimist on the established players in the market because I look around and I say, where's all this stuff you promised? And that's my big beef. Where is it?

Where's all these wonderful multiplayer games you're gonna write? Where where's all these applications you were gonna do? Where's all these, wonderful, oh yeah, you're gonna tokenize assets, aren't you? Where are they? I mean, how long do we have to wait for this stuff?

And I think that is the core problem of crypto is that it's stunted. It's been stunted by the lack of regulatory clarity. And that stunting has meant that there's not really much new in since the last round.

And the round before there was a round I think it's 2018, if memory serves me right, when all these new things were exploding before, basically, regulators woke up and went, woah. Hold on a minute. This is this is getting out of control here. And stamped on everybody. And they did that all around the world. And that has snuffed out innovation.

I think it will come back now that the Trump administration are basically untying Prometheus. And I think that's good because regulation by enforcement, which is what the previous lot had. And you can understand why.

I mean, it's absolutely obvious why, governments hate crypto. I mean, private sector money. Oh, dear. You know, they've got the monopoly of violence and cash, and it violates the monopoly of cash, doesn't it? So they are gonna get a little bit on tip of your toes about that.

Actually making it impossible or nearly impossible for companies to move into blockchain and actually do innovation or do anything at all. I mean, if you have blockchain, banks will close your account. They will literally if you wanna open a bank account, there'll be a little box saying, are you doing anything with crypto or blockchain? And if you tick it, you don't get an account.

So in that environment, you are not gonna get much innovation. You are not gonna get much flourishing of of of that industry. But with a bit of luck, that's all changing in the US now, and they will provide a a infrastructure for people to be in blockchain and in crypto and enable there to be innovation and progress and a flowering of the capabilities of blockchain, which are vast.

And, up until now, only people that are able to really take on all the risk you throw at them and really don't care about any rules and regulations or just happen to be there before they got anybody's attention. They're the only people that that could possibly play. Who would wanna be sued by the SEC for five years? Who can afford to be sued?

So that change should lead over the next few years to to really great strides forward for crypto, if you wanna class it as that. Blockchain, if you wanna class it as that. Crypto and blockchain, if you wanna separate the two. So I think that's really, really good for for blockchain and what becomes crypto.

But it won't be about the next currency. It won't be all about gambling. It won't be all about, when moon, it will be about, oh, I can prove my identity. Oh, I can actually have people pay me to look at their adverts? Oh, I can actually do a transfer of my property on the blockchain in ten minutes rather than ten weeks, and all those sort of things.

And while there are huge barriers in the way, one of them won't be the fear of having your bank account shut down or getting stopped at the airport or having the SEC land 50 lawyers on you.

RS: So how do you choose to be exposed to crypto or blockchain? And what are your thoughts about ETFs and how investors, let's say your average retail investors, how they may choose or think about being exposed to that part of things?

CC: I got an epiphany before it became a capital crime. So in 2015, 2016, I went, holy cow. This is it. As I said, throughout those cycles, it did really well for me. Investing in various coins and stuff like that.

I'm an investor and a trader, but I think trading is more important when it comes to crypto. It has been in the past. It's been very, very good to me. And for that, I'm very grateful. So that's how I came to it.

Or as I might summarize it, I got into it when I was too smart to realize it. It's probably not such a great idea. But, anyway, it had and has worked out for me. But, I mean, now my appetite is very, very dull, for various crypto ideas.

Although I collect NFTs because I'm stupid, I'm not the sort of person that thinks that Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) gonna go to a million a coin without inflation running really high. The blockchain is now much more interesting to me.

And I'm not interested in what moon, I'm not interested in pump.fun. I'm not interested in any of that gambling stuff. I'm just not interested in that at all even though I probably could do okay on it. Because, ultimately, I think that that that is not where the big opportunity lies.

If you can make a product that maybe doesn't even involve a a crypto token per se, or certainly not one where the price is gonna move or that's gonna be part of the tradition of the way that people get excited about crypto, but actually has a really strong use case that involves a blockchain which is permissionless, etcetera, all that good stuff, then I think that is where the next leg up for blockchain is.

That's where the next value add is. That's where the next multibillion dollar businesses will come.

RS: So not a fan of the ETFs?

CC: Well, ETFs I mean, look. Bitcoin is just another asset. I mean, why not palladium? Why not platinum?

Why not gold? Why not silver? Why not corn? Why not an inverse Jim Cramer speculatory ETF, levered. What's the difference? It's just another asset.

I think that's my feeling about Bitcoin at the moment. It's just another asset. It's not one with a crazy upside attached to it. It's got no more or less upside to it than gold or Intel (INTC) or a bottle of wine from somewhere. Or maybe not the bottle of wine because you drink it, wouldn't you?

But you see what I'm saying? It's no longer a phenomena. It's over. It's connected to the infrastructure of the financial system, which after all was meant to be the antidote for it rather than to be seconded there.

And now that Wall Street has got its magic plugged into it, it will do what Wall Street does, which is normalize it, embrace it, extend it, and, consume it. The ETF was never gonna be a good thing. Never gonna be a good thing and isn't a good thing.

And if anything, it will hold the price down. I mean, people are quite happy to say that gold has been suppressed by Wall Street for decades. They're happy to say that. Happy to believe that. Why would it be any different for Bitcoin?

data.title: RISR: This High Yield ETF Thrives In Rising Rates
data.description:
RISR strategy

FolioBeyond Alternative Income and Interest Rate Hedge ETF (NYSEARCA:RISR) is an actively managed ETF launched on 9/30/2021. RISR has a 30-day SEC yield of 6.32%, a trailing 12-month yield of 5.60% and an expense ratio of 1.23%. Distributions are paid monthly. As described by Folio Beyond, the fund primarily invests in interest-only mortgage-backed securities (“MBS IOs”) and U.S. Treasury bonds.

Separating the principal and interest on a debt obligation is known as "stripping". The interest-only (“IO”) strip represents the interest stream. The principal-only (“PO”) strip represents the portion of the debt payment that is applied to the balance of the loan. In the early years of a mortgage, most of the payment is interest. In later years, the interest portion becomes smaller and more of the payment goes to the principal. When rates rise, mortgage prepayments decline, interest cash flows increase and MBS IO value increases. When rates fall, mortgage prepayments increase, interest cash flows decline and MBS IO value decreases. Therefore, MBS IOs gain in value with rising rates and lose with falling rates, at the opposite of most debt securities. This is known as “negative duration”.

RISR invests in Agency MBS IOs, and uses Treasury bonds and MBS derivatives to mitigate the risk of deflation and target a negative duration of 3 to 10 years, meaning an increase of one basis point in interest rates would lead to an increase of portfolio value of 3 to 10 basis points. The portfolio turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 31%.

Portfolio

The effective duration of the portfolio measured as of 4/30/2025 is -4.6 years. RISR is invested in securities backed by agencies (Freddie Mac, Fannie Mae and Ginnie Mae), Treasuries and cash equivalents, therefore credit risk is minimal.

Performance

RISR has a total return over 80% since inception, while a total bond market benchmark (BND), a MBS benchmark (MBB) and a Treasury benchmark (IEF) are all in negative territory.

Nonetheless, keep in mind this period was very favorable to MBS IOs, with the 10-year Treasury yield going up from 1.58% to 4.28%. It is unlikely to continue on the same slope.

The annual sum of monthly distributions has increased between 2022 and 2023, then decreased in 2024, as plotted on the next chart. Distribution history is too short to make long-term generalizations, but obviously we can expect the yield to be sensitive to rate variations.

RISR vs competitors

The next table compares characteristics of RISR and five popular actively managed fixed income ETFs:

  • Dimensional Core Fixed Income ETF (DFCF)
  • JPMorgan Core Plus Bond ETF (JCPB)
  • PIMCO Active Bond Exchange-Traded Fund (BOND)
  • First Trust TCW Opportunistic Fixed Income ETF (FIXD)
  • Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA)

They are mostly invested in MBS and Treasuries, except JAAA, which I took as an example of collateralized loan obligation ETF. For clarity, I have limited the list of competitors to five items. If you think another ETF is worth mentioning, feel free to share your insights in a comment below.

RISR is the best performer by far since November 2021, in both total return and Sharpe ratio (risk-adjusted performance). In fact, RISR has even crushed the Nasdaq 100 Index (QQQ) in this time frame: 78.1% vs. 35.6% in total return! However, this exceptional performance is not representative of the long-term potential of the strategy. It is the result of a perfect timing, RISR being launched just before the most impressive rate increase since the 1980s.

Takeaway

FolioBeyond Alternative Income and Interest Rate Hedge ETF (RISR) has a 5.6% yield from interest-only mortgage-backed securities. It has an astonishing feat for an income ETF: negative duration. RISR has an amazing total return since inception, crushing the Nasdaq 100 itself. It shows the effectiveness of the strategy in rising rates, but cannot be generalized in the long term. We still have to see how RISR behaves in falling rates. Anyway, RISR is not designed to be a standalone long-term investment. It is better used as a hedge against rate risk in a fixed income portfolio, a hedge against inflation in general, or a trading instrument in expectation of rising rates. The main inconvenient of RISR is a high expense ratio, maybe justified by its uniqueness.
